course_description:
This is a targeted preparatory course for all students enrolled in the Master of Educational Studies program. It is intended to develop foundational understandings of educational theories that relate to teaching and learning in a globalised context, develop a working knowledge of research methodologies and underpinning ethical issues, enable the acquisition of writing skills for different genres and audiences, further the development of collaborative learning in cross cultural contexts and strengthen students' analytical skills. The course will introduce students to key educational theories, build an understanding of policy and its relation to curriculum and pedagogy, and develop understandings of the role of language and literacy with a particular focus on the globalisation of English.

This course offers a rich range of content and learning opportunities. It examines both the challenges and benefits of globalisation’s impact on education and invites students to analyse these issues through a critical lens.

Each week, the lecturer will deliver an interactive lecture that introduces and explores key topics related to education in a globalised world. These sessions are designed to foster dialogue, encourage questioning, and support critical reflection.

Following the lecture, students will participate in a workshop featuring tailored activities that strengthen academic and analytical skills. The workshop directly connects to the weekly content and assessment tasks, offering practical opportunities to apply concepts, deepen understanding, and refine academic approaches.

course_aims: The course aims broadly to enable students enrolled in postgraduate programs in Educational Studies to develop and demonstrate foundational understandings and critical analysis of concepts, contexts and issues in Global Education. The course also focuses on developing students’ practical knowledge in the academic skills necessary for the completion of their postgraduate studies.

learning_outcomes.description: Demonstrate foundational understandings and critical analysis of educational concepts and issues that relate to teaching and learning in a globalised context.
Demonstrate foundational understandings of educational research and underpinning ethical issues.
Demonstrate working knowledge of the academic skills expected in the postgraduate programs in Educational Studies.
Demonstrate collaborative learning skills in cross-cultural contexts.

assessment_details.title: Weekly Journal (Reflection)
assessment_details.task_description: Between weeks 1-5, students will submit five weekly journal entries via Blackboard (Bb). The weekly journals contain reflections on week 1 to 5 learning. The purpose of this task is for you to reflect about important aspects of what we are covering/learning in lectures and readings. Each week you may like to spend 10 minutes after lecture to answer the following questions: What was the most important thing/concept/idea you learned during the lecture? What was something you already knew or had learned but it was reinforced? What are your beliefs about teaching and learning in global contexts? Has your belief changed because of the way you have been taught to think critically? Or do you still have the same belief about teaching/learning? What are the implications of learning of this important concept/idea for your future role as educator? Please summarise your reflection and consider how you may be able to apply what we learn in class to your career and/or to your other subjects in the Master of Educational Studies. There is no need to write answers to each of the questions. These questions are supposed to serve as a guide for your reflection. Read full task description Hide full task description
assessment_details.submission_guidelines:
Submission instructions will be given in class in week 1 and will also be available on Blackboard.
Deferral or extension
You cannot defer or apply for an extension for this assessment.
Weekly submissions should be received within respective weeks. If students require an extension for this task due to unavoidable reasons (such as late enrolment), please contact your tutor or course coordinator directly.
Late submission
A penalty of 1 grade for each 24 hour period from time submission is due will apply for up to 7 days. After 7 days you will receive a mark of 0.
assessment_details.deferral_or_extension: You cannot defer or apply for an extension for this assessment.
assessment_details.late_submission: A penalty of 1 grade for each 24 hour period from time submission is due will apply for up to 7 days. After 7 days you will receive a mark of 0.

assessment_details.title: Argumentative Presentation
assessment_details.task_description: For this assessment, you will prepare and deliver a 20‑minute oral presentation on one of the assigned topics. Your presentation will be followed by a question‑and‑answer session with your peers and your lecturer/tutor. As part of your participation, you are required to ask at least one constructive question during a classmate’s presentation. Your engagement as an audience member will also contribute to your mark. The presentation topics focus on key challenges related to the globalisation of education. They are drawn from the central ideas, debates, and issues explored in course readings, lectures, and learning activities. You will be given a pool of topics, and one will be allocated to you during the workshop. Your presentation must take a clear position and use a persuasive, argumentative tone. To prepare effectively, ensure your presentation includes: 1. Introduction Introduce the topic and outline the main debates or issues that shape your argument. 2. Persuasive language features 3. Key Concepts and Theories Explain the central concepts and/or theoretical frameworks relevant to your topic. 4. Engagement with Literature Include a minimum of 10 academic references: 6 sources from the course readings, and 4 additional sources from outside the reading list. 5. Main Argument Present a clear, well‑supported argument that responds directly to the topic. 6. Reflection Reflect on your experience preparing the presentation, including any challenges you encountered and the benefits gained. 7. Implications Discuss any theoretical and/or pedagogical implications that arise from your argument. Each presentation will be 20 minutes long. From this, 5 minutes will be reserved for questions from the audience and/or tutor. Presentations will be delivered live: in class for on-campus students and online for external students. Recorded presentations will not be accepted. You may use PowerPoint slides. Presentations longer than 23 minutes may be penalised. Presentations will begin in Week 3. All presentations will be recorded for assessment purposes. The topic will be shared with students in Week 1. Read full task description Hide full task description
assessment_details.submission_guidelines:
Students will deliver their presentations in class during the workshop session.
Deferral or extension
You may be able to apply for an extension .
The maximum extension allowed is 28 days. Extensions are given in multiples of 24 hours.
Due to the tight scheduling of this assessment, students are not permitted to change or swap, their presentation dates. Speak to the course Coordinator directly regarding this. If a student requires an extension to this task they must request one via myRequests at myUQ.
Late submission
A penalty of 1 grade for each 24 hour period from time submission is due will apply for up to 7 days. After 7 days you will receive a mark of 0.
assessment_details.deferral_or_extension: You may be able to apply for an extension .
assessment_details.late_submission: A penalty of 1 grade for each 24 hour period from time submission is due will apply for up to 7 days. After 7 days you will receive a mark of 0.

assessment_details.title: Project Report
assessment_details.task_description: Throughout the semester, you will conduct an interview with one of your classmates about their experiences of teaching and learning (or learning only, if your partner does not have teaching experience). When developing your interview questions, consider how educational experiences are shaped by specific national contexts, as well as the influence of globalised factors on local educational practices. You may also choose to explore your partner’s educational experiences abroad (for example, studying in Australia). Guidance and practice for conducting interviews will be provided in class. After completing the interview, you will write a report that includes both a summary of your partner’s responses and an analysis of these responses using concepts and theories covered in the course. In your analysis, you may focus on a single concept/theory or draw on several. For instance, you might examine how globalised educational policies shape individual experiences of teaching and learning. Your report must also include a reflective section in which you analyse your own educational experiences and contexts, drawing comparisons with your partner’s interview data. Report structure: Your report should include the following sections: 1. Introduction · State the aims and focus of your project. 2. Methods · Describe the interview process, including the techniques/methods used to collect data. · You may also include your data analysis approach (optional). 3. Summary of Interview Responses · Present a clear and concise overview of your partner’s responses. 4. Analysis · Analyse the interview data using relevant concepts and theories from the course. 5. Reflective Section · Reflect on your own educational experiences and contexts. · Draw comparisons between your experiences and your partner’s interview data. 6. Conclusion · Summarise the key insights from your project. 7. Reference List · Include all sources cited in your report, using the APA referencing style. 8. Appendices · Include your interview questions and transcript. · A transcript may be omitted if you provide a working link to the interview recording. · You may also include any other relevant materials used in the project. Further guidance on report writing will be provided in class. Read full task description Hide full task description
